summary: This post benchmarks dask's out-of-core matrix multiplication capabilities, comparing performance between reference BLAS and OpenBLAS. The key finding is that reading large matrices from disk doesn't significantly hurt performance for compute-bound operations like matrix multiplication, achieving 18.9 GFLOPS from disk versus 6 GFLOPS in-memory with reference BLAS. However, combining dask's parallelization with OpenBLAS's multi-threading actually hurts performance, suggesting users should avoid using two parallelization mechanisms simultaneously. The post serves as an early proof-of-concept for dask's ability to handle datasets larger than memory.
key_points: Matrix multiplication is compute-bound (n³ operations for n² bytes read), making I/O relatively cheap compared to computation
Out-of-core dask achieved 18.9 GFLOPS versus 6 GFLOPS for in-memory NumPy with reference BLAS, due to parallel core utilization
OpenBLAS provides ~4x speedup over reference BLAS for in-memory operations
Combining dask parallelization with OpenBLAS multi-threading causes performance degradation rather than improvement
These results only apply to highly compute-bound operations and shouldn't be generalized to I/O-bound workloads
money_quotes: It's as if all of our hard drive just became memory.
When doing compute intensive work, don't worry about using disk, just don't use two mechanisms of parallelism at the same time.
We don't lose much by operating from disk in compute-intensive tasks.
When multiplying two n×n matrices we read n² bytes but perform n³ computations. There are n computations to do per byte so, relatively speaking, I/O is cheap.
Dask doesn't benefit much from an optimized BLAS. This is sad and surprising.
key_insight: For compute-intensive operations, disk-based out-of-core computation can match or exceed in-memory performance through parallelization, but users must avoid layering multiple parallelization strategies.
